title: Equipment Spacing Guide
summary: Understand rack rows, rack dimensions, hot/cold aisle spacing, end clearance, and serviceable room layout.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the physical area required for rack rows and access clearances based on rack quantity, rack dimensions, aisle depth, and end clearance.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid before the layout, rack, pathway, or support-room decision gets locked in.
Use it to document the assumptions behind a physical infrastructure decision.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble or rework.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when laying out equipment rooms, small data rooms, network closets, security head-end spaces, rack rows, or any area where access and airflow matter.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, installer, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on the Equipment Spacing Planner and explain how each value affects the physical layout.

items.label: Rack Rows
items.description: The number of rack rows included in the layout.
items.whyItMatters: More rows increase the layout footprint and change aisle planning.
items.commonMistake: Counting rack positions without considering row-to-row spacing.

items.label: Racks per Row
items.description: The number of racks or cabinets placed in each row.
items.whyItMatters: Rack count drives total row width and can affect aisle, service, and cable pathway requirements.
items.commonMistake: Planning rack count without checking usable room width.

items.label: Rack Width (in)
items.description: The width of each rack or cabinet in inches.
items.whyItMatters: Rack width determines row length and clearance needs.
items.commonMistake: Assuming all cabinets are the same width without checking actual model dimensions.

items.label: Rack Depth (in)
items.description: The front-to-back depth of each rack or cabinet in inches.
items.whyItMatters: Rack depth affects hot/cold aisle spacing and total room depth.
items.commonMistake: Using equipment depth but forgetting rack frame, rear cabling, and door clearance.

items.label: Cold Aisle (in)
items.description: The planned front-side working or intake aisle clearance.
items.whyItMatters: Cold aisle clearance affects service access, airflow behavior, and safe work space.
items.commonMistake: Shrinking the front aisle until equipment doors or technicians do not have enough room.

items.label: Hot Aisle (in)
items.description: The planned rear-side service or exhaust aisle clearance.
items.whyItMatters: Rear clearance affects cable access, exhaust airflow, and maintenance work.
items.commonMistake: Forgetting rear access because the front of the rack looks reachable.

items.label: End Clearance (in)
items.description: Clearance at the end of rack rows for access, turning, doors, or equipment movement.
items.whyItMatters: End clearance prevents a layout from becoming physically difficult to access or service.
items.commonMistake: Using every inch of room width and leaving no end access.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the layout has practical service space. Watch means aisle or end clearance assumptions should be checked. Risk means the layout may be too tight for safe access, airflow, or maintenance.
bullets: Healthy: the rack layout has usable aisle and service margin.
Watch: confirm door swing, equipment depth, cable access, and service procedures.
Risk: reduce rack count, increase room size, or revise the layout before installation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Infrastructure planning problems usually come from treating the room, rack, pathway, power support, and service space as separate decisions. In the field, those limits stack together.
bullets: Sizing only the equipment and forgetting clearance, access, maintenance, and growth.
Using nominal dimensions without adding real cable, airflow, door swing, service, and replacement space.
Assuming a rack, floor, conduit, tray, UPS room, or generator can be pushed to the edge without operational side effects.
Failing to document assumptions before the layout becomes hard or expensive to change.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer installation documents, structural review, electrical design, local code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits and installation instructions still matter.
Local code, AHJ direction, and site standards may change the final requirement.
Existing rooms, pathways, and support infrastructure can create limits that simple math does not show.
A design with reserve space and documented assumptions is usually easier to install, support, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, code compliance review, field measurement, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual room, rack, pathway, equipment, and site conditions.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, service access, installation constraints, and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
